narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 34, a middle-income family background, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, designed to predict first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 57.6% chance that you would fail the bar—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as schools prioritize applicants likely to pass and thrive in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, which stood out starkly compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance and signaled potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. Your first-year decile, also at the bottom and well below average, reinforced this concern as the next most influential issue, suggesting early academic hurdles that often persist. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, though below the dataset average of 36.7, pulled strongly in the direction of passing, showing your aptitude for logical reasoning. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly under the average 3.21, offered some positive counterbalance but wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Your middle-income background and full-time enrollment plans both nudged the prediction toward success, aligning with profiles of students who typically fare better. Compared to typical students—who boast higher decile rankings and stronger test scores—your early academic standings were the key divergences that tipped the scales. While this decision stings, it's not the end of your path; consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence, strengthening your recent grades through targeted courses, or exploring part-time programs or gap-year experiences that highlight your resilience. Many trailblazing Black women lawyers have overcome similar predictions by addressing these exact gaps—your determination can rewrite this story. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger.
